title: The Gradient Vector
concept: All gradients together form a gradient vector, a single direction in weight space that tells the whole model how to move to reduce loss.

exercises.prompt: Recap: After backprop gives one weight a gradient, what does the optimizer do next?

options.text: It uses that gradient to nudge the weight in a better direction.
It turns the gradient into a new training label.
It removes that weight from the network.

exercises.explain: Right. A gradient is useful because it guides the next update step.

cards.title: One arrow, many knobs
cards.body: Imagine a giant spaceship control panel with thousands of sliders. After one flight mistake, the autopilot must decide how to adjust the whole panel, not just one slider. The gradient vector is that combined instruction: one overall arrow for the model to follow.

cards.title: What is a gradient vector?
cards.body: A neural network has many weights, and each weight gets its own gradient. If you stack all those gradients together, you get a gradient vector: one big list that describes the model's best downhill direction across all weights at once.

cards.title: Walking with many controls
cards.body: If you are walking on a hill, one step direction is enough when the ground has only two visible directions. But a neural network lives in a world with far more directions because every weight is another control. The vector is like combining every tiny instruction into one travel direction.

cards.title: Three weights, one move
cards.body: Suppose a tiny model has three weights. Backprop returns gradients like 'lower weight 1 a bit, raise weight 2 a bit, barely touch weight 3.' Those three suggestions together make one update direction. The optimizer follows that whole package, not three unrelated guesses.

cards.title: Not just a pile of independent fixes
cards.body: It is tempting to think of training as thousands of separate mini-repairs that never relate. In practice, the optimizer treats them as one coordinated move through a huge space. The model's next step is a team move.

cards.title: Single control vs. many controls
cards.body: Both cases use gradients, but neural networks usually update many weights together.

left.text:
One gradient gives one simple instruction.
Example: just lower one dial.

right.text:
All gradients combine into one vector.
The model moves through a much larger space.

exercises.prompt: Which description best matches a gradient vector?

options.text: The full collection of weight gradients, treated as one direction for the model update.
A list of training examples sorted by difficulty.
A copy of the loss function before training starts.
The number of neurons in the output layer only.

exercises.explain: Correct. A gradient vector bundles all weight gradients into one overall move direction.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If one weight has a zero gradient, that means the rest of the network has no update direction either.
exercises.explain: False. One weight can stay still while many others still have meaningful gradients inside the full vector.

exercises.sentence: A gradient vector combines update signals for all the model's ___.
exercises.explain: Exactly. Every trainable weight contributes one part of the gradient vector.

exercises.prompt: Which option looks most like a gradient vector idea?

options.text: A model gets many weight-specific slopes, then uses them together as one update direction.
A model keeps only the single biggest gradient and throws the rest away.
A model changes labels instead of weights.

exercises.explain: Yes. The key idea is combined movement across many weights, not changing just one thing or editing labels.

exercises.prompt: A tiny network has four weights. Backprop says: weight A should go down a bit, B should go up a bit, C should barely move, and D should go down more strongly. What does the gradient vector represent here?

options.text: One combined direction made from all four suggestions, telling the whole model how to move next.
Only the change for weight D, because it is the biggest.
A command to retrain from scratch using new labels.
Proof that only one weight matters in neural networks.

exercises.explain: Exactly. The gradient vector is the coordinated update direction formed by all the weight-level signals together.
